docs.title: χαῖρε!
docs.text: Logion is an open-source, no-code application designed to assist philologists using state-of-the-art language models. With Logion, researchers can download a suite of pre-modern Greek language models for conducting philological research tasks, such as error word detection and gap filling. Logion is designed to be run on personal computers without access to high performance computing (HPC) resources.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Logion is currently available for Windows and MacOS. We hope to make the application publicly available for download on our website soon.

See the installation instructions for help installing Logion on your device.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Logion requires no outside software or installations.

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text: Logion is developed through collaboration with Princeton University Research Computing and Classics.

docs.title: περῐ́ τῆς μηχᾰνῆς
docs.text: Hardware affects software. Logion does not require access to high-performance computing (HPC) resources. It is designed to run on local machines with diverse hardware and operating systems. Although hardware will not affect the accuracy or quality of Logion's predictions, hardware will affect Logion's speed. As such, for perhaps the first time in history, philologists may want to be aware of their computer's hardware. The following provides a brief overview and guide to help users navigate hardware questions specifically in relation to Logion.

docs.title: Which hardware is best?
docs.text: Hardware will not affect the accuracy or quality of predictions generated by Logion models. But hardware will affect the speed at which Logion generates predictions.

docs.title: CPU vs. GPU
docs.text: Nearly every computer nowadays has both a central processing unit (CPU) and graphics processing unit (GPU). Both are processors that use memory to store/access data, perform calculations, and execute instructions. CPUs are responsible for general-purpose computing tasks (word processors, web browsing, etc.); GPUs are responsible for computationally expensive, high-throughput tasks (video games, training AI models, etc.).

docs.title: Does Logion use a CPU or GPU?
docs.text:
Logion is designed to run on both a local machine's CPU and GPU. When a user starts Logion, the software detects which type of GPU is available on the host machine. If the machine has a compatible GPU, it utilizes that GPU to generate any predictions. If a GPU is unavailable, Logion defaults to the machine's CPU. Logion is compatible with the following types of GPUs:

  • Nvidia
  • Apple Silicon (M-chip)
  • Intel Graphics

Our team's internal experiments suggest that, when executing Logion's error detection task on a sequence of 122 words in Byzantine Greek, CPUs on both Mac and Windows devices require about 20 minutes of processing time. By comparison, an Apple M-chip requires about 8 minutes of processing time.

docs.title: How do I find which GPU my computer has?
On macOS
docs.text:
1. Go to the top-left corner of your computer's menu bar.
  2. Click the Apple Logo ().
  3. Select About This Mac from the drop-down menu.
  4. Your computer's GPU should appear beside "Graphics" or "Chip".

docs.title: On Windows
docs.text:
1. Right-click on the taskbar.
  2. Select Task Manager.
  3. Click the Performance tab in the Task Manager window.
  4. Scroll down and select GPU 0.
  5. GPU information for your computer should appear in the top-right corner.

docs.title: How Logion Works
docs.text: Logion uses language models to make suggestions for missing or alternative words in pre-modern Greek texts.

docs.title: Chance-confidence ratio
docs.text: Logion's error detection algrothim utilizes what is called the chance-confidence ratio. This is an original algorithm developed by Logion team members and first introduced in this ACL paper. The alogirthm determines the likelihood that a given word was mistranscribed at a certain point in the textual history.'

docs.title: What is Levenshtein distance?
docs.text: Levenshtein distance is a term from natural language processing (NLP) that refers to the minimum number of alterations needed to transform one word into another.

docs.title: How does Logion use Levenshtein distance?
docs.text: As such, the selected Levenshtein distance will here limit the model's suggestions to words that have that many character/letter differences from words in the original input text.

docs.title: Error detection
docs.text: Logion's error detection feature is aims to suggest the likelihood that each word in a given sequence is an error, i.e. was mis-transcribed at some point in the textual history. It further offers suggestions as to what the "correct" word may be.

docs.title: How to use Logion error detection
docs.text:
From the main menu, go to to to the error detection window by clicking "Error Detection" on the right-hand side of the main menu. Once you are in the "Error Detection" page, follow these steps to generate an error report for your text.

  1. Select a model from the drop-down menu in the upper-left of the window. If this is your first time, we recommend beginning with Base BERT. This has been trained on a wide selection of pre-modern Greek and is suitable for general error-detection recommendations.

  2. Select a Levenshtein distance from the drop-down menu to the right of the model selection menu. We recommend starting with a Levenshtein distance of 1. For more on what is Levenshtein distance, see our (technical explainer page on the topic)[../explainers/lev-dist.md].

  3. Type/paste your text into the text area. Unlike, Logion's word prediction feature, do NOT use ? to represent any missing words. Enter text comprised of only Greek characters and other punctuation marks.

  4. Click the blue "Detect Errors" button below the text area. Note the error detection process can take several minutes depending on one's local hardware. To read more on how hardware affects error detection processing speed, click here.

Results are displayed below the blue Detect Errors button.

docs.title: How to read error reports
docs.text: Text is color-coded to signify each given word's likelihood of it having been mis-transcribed at some point in the textual history. Text is colored on a gradient of green-yellow-orange-red. Green means the word is unlikely to be mis-transcribed; red means the word is very likely to be mis-transcribed. To see what the model suggests as a potential replacement word, click on a given word. Replacement word suggestions are displayed on the right-hand side of the window beside the model's projected (chance-confidence ratio)[../explainers/ccr.md] for that word-pair. To see a different word's results, simply click on that different word.

docs.title: Installation instructions
docs.text:
For the time being, only select researchers who have been provided a Google Drive link can download the beta version Logion application. After you have accessed the Google Drive folder via a link provided by a Logion team member, follow these instructions to download and install the application:

  1. Hover to the version of Logion corresponding to your device's operating system.

    1. logion-win is for Windows.
    2. logion-mac is for MacOS.

  2. Click the ⋮ icon and select Download from the drop-down menu.

  3. After the .zip file has downloaded, go to your Downlaods folder and double-click the Logion .zip file. This will unzip the application into a new folder.

  4. Inside the resultant folder, double-click the Logion application. This will open and install Logion on our system.

If your institution restricts installation on devices, you may need to contact your IT administrator to temporarily enable installation permissions on your device.

docs.title: How to Use Logion Word Prediction
docs.text:
From the main menu, go to to to the word prediction window by clicking "Word prediction" on the left-hand side of the main menu. Once you are in the "Word Prediction" page, follow these steps to generate an single-word predictions for gaps in your text.

  1. Select a model from the drop-down menu in the upper-left of the window. If this is your first time, we recommend beginning with Base BERT. This has been trained on a wide selection of pre-modern Greek and is suitable for general gap-filling tasks.

  2. Type/paste your text into the text area. Use "?" to represent any missing words for which you want to generate suggestions. Enter text comprised of only Greek characters and other punctuation marks.

  3. Click the blue "Predict" button below the text area. This may take several seconds, particularly if this is your first time using the app or a given model. The model will display its word suggestions with their projected probabilities on the right-hand side of the window.
